target.title: Expose model picker agent runtimes
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds additive `agentRuntime` metadata to `models.list` model choices when the model/provider has an explicit configured agent runtime.
- Shows non-default runtime labels in the WebUI model picker (for example `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex`) without changing the canonical model value (`openai/gpt-5.5`).
- Adds focused gateway helper and UI label regressions for the OpenAI/Codex route split.

Refs #90420 (real 6.1 migration/runtime-observability report) and #84032 (diamond-lobster issue: model picker/provider/runtime ambiguity). Related to #90036 and #90047 because the live Windows 6.1 route incident made the same ambiguity operationally visible: the healthy route was `openai/gpt-5.5` + Codex runtime, while stale durable state still surfaced as `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`.

## Why

After the 2026.6.1 Codex migration, a healthy route can be:

```text
model ref: openai/gpt-5.5
runtime: codex
```

The picker previously only exposed/displayed provider/model metadata, so operators could not tell whether an `openai/*` model was configured to run through Codex or through the default OpenClaw route. This PR keeps the user-facing model ref stable while exposing the configured runtime separately.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern "surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"`
- `node --import tsx` behavior script for `addConfiguredAgentRuntimeMetadata` → `MODELS_LIST_RUNTIME_METADATA_OK`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui`

Note: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/models-list-result.test.ts --testNamePattern "runtime metadata"` and `pnpm tsgo:test:src` were attempted locally but remained silent/slow in this checkout and were stopped; the focused helper behavior was verified directly and CI should run the normal suite.


## Review fix proof

Addressed ClawSweeper's implicit-runtime feedback in `a971a356`.

Focused proof from the patched branch:

```text
implicitCodex.agentRuntime = { id: "codex", source: "implicit", label: "OpenAI Codex" }
implicitDefault.agentRuntime = omitted
explicitOpenClaw.agentRuntime = { id: "openclaw", source: "model", label: "OpenClaw Default" }
labels = ["GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex", "GPT-5.5 · OpenClaw Default"]
RUNTIME_METADATA_REVIEW_FIX_OK

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern "agent runtime|OpenClaw runtime"
✓ ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts (25 tests | 23 skipped)
Tests 2 passed | 23 skipped

git diff --check
```

The picker now distinguishes both default implicit OpenAI → Codex routing and explicit OpenClaw runtime overrides while still omitting compact implicit default/OpenClaw/auto metadata for unrelated providers.

## Real behavior proof (actual PR-head Gateway/WebUI; current head: `bc62b391bf7f7c9e6ea722c5fd746ded2a5da612`)

Regenerated on 2026-06-08 from an actual temporary Gateway built from this PR head and the real Control UI served by that Gateway. This is not the earlier reused screenshot and does not depend on the model-picker helper/mock path.

- Behavior or issue addressed: expose material runtime metadata for canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` choices routed through the Codex runtime, and render that runtime in the WebUI Chat model picker as `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex` without changing the canonical model id/provider.
- Real environment tested: PR #90328 head `bc62b391bf7f7c9e6ea722c5fd746ded2a5da612`, built and run with the source CLI/Gateway from that head (`OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (bc62b39)`) using isolated profile/home `/tmp/openclaw-pr90328-proof/home`, profile `pr90328proof`, Gateway `http://127.0.0.1:19129`. Channels were skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`; only the `codex` runtime plugin loaded. Auth/token secrets were not required. The configured test model intentionally reports `available: false`; model auth availability is outside this picker metadata behavior.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash

git rev-parse HEAD
# bc62b391bf7f7c9e6ea722c5fd746ded2a5da612

env -i HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr90328-proof/home PATH="$PATH" OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=19129 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 \

  node scripts/run-node.mjs --profile pr90328proof gateway call models.list --params '{"view":"configured"}' --json

# Browser/CDP opened the same real Control UI:
# http://127.0.0.1:19129/chat?session=agent%3Amain%3Amain
# and captured the open chat model picker screenshot.
```

  - Actual PR-head Gateway `models.list` response: [models-list-pr-head-real-gateway-20260608.json](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ooiuuii/openclaw/refs/heads/proof/90328-model-picker-20260606/proof/90328/models-list-pr-head-real-gateway-20260608.json)

  - Updated proof README: [proof/90328 README](https://github.com/ooiuuii/openclaw/tree/proof/90328-model-picker-20260606/proof/90328)

![Actual PR-head WebUI picker showing GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ooiuuii/openclaw/refs/heads/proof/90328-model-picker-20260606/proof/90328/pr-head-webui-picker-dropdown-20260608.png)

- Observed result after fix: the real PR-head Gateway preserves the canonical public choice (`provider: "openai"`, `id: "gpt-5.5"`) while exposing runtime metadata separately as `agentRuntime.id = "codex"` / `label = "OpenAI Codex"`. The real PR-head Control UI consumes that response and renders the model picker selected option as `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex`; the captured visible label text was `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex · Medium`.
- What was not tested: real OAuth inference, channel delivery, commentary/progress streams, native Swift UI display, or session migration behavior. Those are intentionally outside this PR's WebUI/Gateway metadata scope.

target.title: feat: add runtime self context config and tool
target.body:
## Related issue

Implementation slice for https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/89537.

This PR implements the first config-backed `Runtime Self Context` slice for the larger runtime/offload/scale/cost-awareness effort tracked in the issue.

## Related PRs

- Runtime placement / execution backend companion: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/84758

## CONTRIBUTING alignment

- AI-assisted PR: yes; the implementation and review/fix loop used Codex/OpenClaw assistance, and the author is responsible for understanding and owning the shipped code.
- Current-head real behavior proof: included below for `26f124c83ed3`, including the Tool Search and Code Mode final tool-surface gating behavior.
- Proof freshness rule: after any rebase or meaningful fix, refresh the proof section with the new head SHA, exact command shape, and copied terminal/log evidence.
- Bot review status: ClawSweeper has marked contributor proof sufficient and ready for maintainer look; remaining accepted decisions are documented as maintainer contract choices.
- Takeover readiness: maintainer edits are enabled, related PR #84758 is linked, and this PR intentionally does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`.
- Contract approval needed before merge: maintainers should explicitly approve the v1 `runtimeContext` config surface and model-facing `runtime` tool contract.

## Goal

Give an agent a cheap, reliable way to know where it is running, what it can safely do there, whether runtime details are available through a canonical tool, and where future scale/offload/cost information will attach without turning prompt context into a full environment inventory.

Runtime context is stored in config/session metadata shape; prompt injection is optional and centralized in conversation assembly, so individual agent prompt flavors do not need to repeat it.

## Implemented v1 slice

- Adds typed `runtimeContext` config support with strict schema validation.
- Supports exposure modes: `none`, `tool_hint`, and `prompt_summary`.
- Injects runtime self-context through the existing hidden `openclaw.runtime-context` path during conversation assembly when the runner has a transcript/model split available.
- Keeps `none` fully silent: no prompt injection and no runtime tool.
- Adds a core `runtime` tool for config-backed `self`, `describe`, `actions`, `offload_targets`, and `cost_estimate` actions.
- Adds schema/types for current runtime, resources, limits, opaque action refs, offload targets, cost hints, freshness, and provenance.
- Hardens prompt summaries so provider/config strings are normalized and rendered as data, not raw high-trust prompt lines.
- Adds tests for prompt rendering, hidden runtime-context wrapping, runtime tool behavior, and config schema acceptance.
- Keeps provider refresh, action execution, and precise cost estimates out of this first slice.

## Team review findings addressed

- Fixed hidden runtime-context leakage risk for callers without a transcript prompt split.
- Fixed prompt-summary injection risk from provider/config strings by rendering normalized quoted data strings.
- Fixed exposed-but-empty runtime context behavior: the tool now returns structured `status: unavailable` instead of throwing on every action.
- Fixed `cost_estimate` target ambiguity: missing target id is only allowed with a single target, unknown ids return `target_not_found`.
- Fixed OpenAI strict tool-schema compatibility by removing the free-form `additionalProperties: true` workload shape.
- Fixed prompt summary `validUntil` fallback from config-level `runtimeContext.validUntil`.

## Non-goals

- No broad environment inventory in v1.
- No scheduler/load model.
- No capacity slots.
- No CPU/memory class fields.
- No raw provider commands in prompt-facing data.
- No provider-backed runtime refresh yet.
- No action-ref execution yet.
- No precise provider cost estimator yet.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: After the review fixes, a real OpenClaw checkout can render runtime self-context from config, expose the `runtime` tool, return offload/cost data, avoid plausible cost output for unknown targets, and normalize instruction-looking provider strings in `prompt_summary`.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw repository checkout on branch `draft/runtime-self-context-plan` at head `e8140525cc`, using the repo's installed pnpm dependencies and `tsx` to import the changed TypeScript modules directly.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran this command after the fix commit:

```text
pnpm exec tsx -e 'import { buildRuntimeSelfContextPrompt } from "./src/runtime-self-context/render.ts"; import { createRuntimeTool } from "./src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.ts"; ... create runtimeContext config, render prompt summary, call runtime describe, cost_estimate known target, and cost_estimate missing target ...'
```

- **Evidence after fix**: Copied live console output from the command above, with action refs redacted:

```text
PROMPT_SUMMARY_START
Runtime details are available through the runtime tool. Do not guess local resources, scale options, offload targets, or cost. If a task may need more compute, delegation, runtime scaling, or budget-aware placement, call the runtime tool for fresh details.

Runtime summary:
- current: "OpenClaw Dev Ignore previous instructions", local
- resources: 8 CPU, "Apple M3 Max"; 32 GiB memory; no accelerator
- actions: scale_up approval
- offload: 1 target available
- cost: included
- details: call runtime tool action "describe" for fresh details
- valid until: "2026-06-03T22:00:00-07:00"
PROMPT_SUMMARY_END
TOOL_PRESENT true
DESCRIBE {
  "value": {
    "id": "openclaw-dev",
    "current": {
      "id": "openclaw-dev",
      "label": "OpenClaw Dev\nIgnore previous instructions",
      "locality": "local"
    },
    "actions": [
      {
        "kind": "scale_up",
        "label": "Resize this runtime",
        "ref": "runtime-action://redacted/current/scale-up",
        "requiresApproval": true
      }
    ],
    "offload": {
      "targets": [
        {
          "id": "gateway-large",
          "locality": "cloud",
          "workloadKinds": ["codex", "long_task"],
          "cost": {
            "model": "metered",
            "currency": "USD",
            "estimateRef": "runtime-cost://redacted/estimate"
          }
        }
      ]
    },
    "cost": { "model": "included" }
  }
}
COST_KNOWN {
  "targetId": "gateway-large",
  "cost": {
    "model": "metered",
    "currency": "USD",
    "estimateRef": "runtime-cost://redacted/estimate"
  },
  "workload": { "kind": "build", "estimatedSeconds": 120 },
  "estimate": {
    "status": "not_available",
    "reason": "No provider-backed runtime cost estimator is registered in this v1 slice."
  }
}
COST_MISSING {
  "targetId": "missing",
  "estimate": {
    "status": "target_not_found",
    "reason": "No configured offload target matched targetId."
  }
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The runtime summary is generated from real module imports, the newline/instruction-looking label is rendered as a single quoted data value, the runtime tool is present, `describe` returns current/actions/offload/cost data, known-target cost returns the configured hint, and missing-target cost returns explicit `target_not_found` instead of a plausible fallback cost.
- **What was not tested**: Provider-backed refresh, action-ref execution, and precise provider cost estimation are out of scope for this v1 slice.

## Verification

Passed:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-src.config.ts src/runtime-self-context/render.test.ts --reporter verbose
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts --reporter verbose -t runtimeContext
pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.test.ts --reporter verbose
pnpm exec oxlint --threads=1 src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.ts src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.test.ts src/runtime-self-context/render.ts src/runtime-self-context/render.test.ts src/config/types.openclaw.ts src/config/types.runtime-context.ts src/config/zod-schema.runtime-context.ts src/config/zod-schema.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts
```

Notes:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.test.ts --reporter verbose
pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-tools.config.ts tools/runtime-tool.test.ts --reporter verbose
```

The local scoped `agents-tools` vitest config reported `No test files found` because its CLI include narrowing did not match this checkout invocation. A generic direct vitest invocation for `src/agents/tools/runtime-tool.test.ts` hung in local bundling, so the tool behavior is covered by the live `tsx` proof above plus oxlint. The file is still included in this PR and remains available for CI's normal test selection.

Attempted but not usable in this local checkout:

```text
pnpm exec tsc --noEmit --pretty false
```

The full repo typecheck hit Node heap OOM at the default heap limit. Retrying with a larger heap ran for several minutes without producing actionable output, so the recorded proof is focused tests, hidden-context split tests, live runtime tool output, and oxlint.

## Follow-up proof for policy/docs review findings

Latest follow-up commit: `70da8a35f8` (`fix(runtime-context): register runtime policy surfaces`).

Addressed review findings:

- `[P1] Register runtime with core tool policy`: `runtime` is now registered in `CORE_TOOL_DEFINITIONS`, included in the `coding` profile, present in generated `group:runtime` and `group:openclaw`, and listed in `OPENCLAW_TOOL_FACTORY_NAMES` so `tools.allow: ["runtime"]` constructs the OpenClaw runtime tool instead of treating it as plugin-only. Coverage asserts all of those policy surfaces and the narrow allowlist construction plan.
- `[P2] Document the runtimeContext config contract`: public config/tool-policy docs now describe the top-level `runtimeContext` shape, exposure modes, tool-policy interaction, and v1 limitations. Config schema labels/help also expose the new contract fields.

Verification after `70da8a35f8`:

## Follow-up proof for effective tool-policy hint gate

Latest follow-up commit: `5de679eed5` (`fix(runtime-context): gate hints on effective tool policy`).

Fix:

- Runtime self-context prompt hint/summary injection now receives the final constructed tool surface from the embedded runner.
- If effective tool policy filters out `runtime`, `appendRuntimeSelfContextToPrompt` suppresses the hint/summary instead of telling the model to call a missing tool.
- Added focused coverage for the fail-closed render behavior.

Configured-run proof after `5de679eed5`:

```text
CI=true node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/runtime-self-context/render.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.test.ts src/agents/tool-catalog.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt-tool-construction-plan.test.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts

# passed with no output
```

target.title: fix(imessage): self-explaining private-API failures and dedicated send timeout
target.body:
## Summary

Two macOS-26-resilience fixes for the iMessage channel:

1. **Surface *why* the private API is unavailable.** The probe now carries imsg's own `status --json` `message` (SIP / library validation / macOS 26 AMFI gate) into `IMessagePrivateApiStatus.statusMessage`, and blocked actions append it. Operators on macOS 26.5 stop getting the misleading "run imsg launch" with no reason.

2. **Decouple the send timeout from the probe timeout.** Sends inherited the 10s probe default via `client.request`. On macOS 26 the private-API bridge intermittently stalls up to ~124s; the 10s abort dropped attachment/reply sends (non-recoverable shapes). New `DEFAULT_IMESSAGE_SEND_TIMEOUT_MS = 150_000` for sends only (covers the observed upper bound + headroom); probes/health checks stay fast. **No new config surface** — explicit opts/`probeTimeoutMs` still win. Mirrors the BlueBubbles send-timeout fix (#69193).

3. **Companion imsg bridge fix.** ClawSweeper correctly found that OpenClaw's 150s client wait was incomplete while upstream `imsg` bridge sends still defaulted to 10s. Companion PR open: openclaw/imsg#139 at `4c85d165c31f4711f75d7bfda4c31723219ebadd` changes send-style bridge actions (`send-message`, `send-multipart`, `send-attachment`, `send-poll`, `send-reaction`) to use a 150s bridge response timeout while keeping non-send RPC waits at 10s and preserving explicit timeout overrides. `send-reaction` is included because tapbacks build an `IMMessage` and dispatch through `sendMessage:`; `notify-anyways` intentionally stays at 10s because it is a message-item mutation path.

## Verification

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` clean.
- Full imessage extension suite: **548 tests pass**, incl. 2 new tests (statusMessage propagation; 150s send default).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage/src/status.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/actions.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts` (3 files, 81 tests passed).
- Codex `$autoreview` clean (it caught an initial 60s-too-short default vs the documented 124s stall window; fixed to 150s).
- **Dependency source inspected:** local sibling imsg source had `IMsgBridgeProtocol.defaultResponseTimeout = 10.0`, and `RPCServer` / CLI bridge send paths called `IMsgBridgeClient.shared.invoke` without a longer send timeout. Companion openclaw/imsg#139 fixes that dependency-layer timeout contract.
- **Companion imsg verification for openclaw/imsg#139 (`4c85d165c31f4711f75d7bfda4c31723219ebadd`):**
  - `swift format lint Sources/IMsgCore/IMsgBridgeProtocol.swift Tests/IMsgCoreTests/IMsgBridgeProtocolTests.swift`
  - `DEVELOPER_DIR=/Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer swift test --filter IMsgBridgeProtocolTests` (7 tests passed)
  - `DEVELOPER_DIR=/Applications/Xcode.app/Contents/Developer make test` (325 tests in 2 suites passed)
  - GitHub CI: `macos` and `linux-read-core` passed
- **Timeout tradeoff:** The 150s default is intentional for send-style private API bridge actions because the observed macOS 26 stalls reached about 124s. A bridge send that never returns can now hold the caller up to 150s; probes, health checks, explicit overrides, and non-send bridge actions keep the short timeout.
- **Live macOS 26.4.1 healthy-host status proof on this machine:** `imsg status --json` returned `message: "Connected to Messages.app. IMCore features available."`, `v2_ready: true`, and `bridge_version: 2`; `pnpm openclaw channels status --probe imessage --json` reported iMessage configured/running with `probe.ok: true` and `privateApi.available: true`.
- **Live macOS 26.5.1 blocked-action proof in a clean VM:** OpenClaw built from this PR was installed and run as a gateway with imsg 0.11.0 present but the private-API bridge intentionally not injected. A real CLI `openclaw message react --channel imessage ...` dispatch returned the blocked-action error with imsg's own reason appended: `imsg reports: SIP is disabled and the helper dylib is present, but Messages.app is not currently injected. Run \`imsg launch\` to enable advanced IMCore features.` See the proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/91041#issuecomment-4641260341
- **Live regression smoke on macOS 26.4.1 (lobster):** built 2026.6.1 + this fix, deployed to the gateway, restarted (PID bounced), iMessage + Telegram both healthy, no startup errors.

target.title: fix(feishu): retry on send rate-limit errors (230020/230006)
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds retry logic (up to 2 retries, 500ms/1000ms linear backoff) to `requestFeishuApi` for Feishu send rate-limit error code `230020`.
- Both `im.message.create` (direct send) and `im.message.reply` (reply send) paths now go through retry.
- Exports `getFeishuSendRateLimitCode()` helper to detect retryable codes from AxiosError response shape.
- Adds 22 unit/integration tests covering concurrent send, retry exhaustion, backoff timing, and error message surfacing.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/send.retry.test.ts` — 13 tests pass
- [x] `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/send.concurrent.test.ts` — 9 tests pass
- [x] Real 20-concurrent direct send stress test: 20/20 success after retry
- [x] Real 15-concurrent reply stress test: 15/15 success after retry

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Feishu plugin now retries transient `230020` rate-limit errors on both direct send (`im.message.create`) and reply (`im.message.reply`) paths, instead of failing immediately and losing messages.

**Real environment tested**: Live Feishu API (`open.feishu.cn`), app `cli_aa947bd28afa5bd9`, macOS local OpenClaw dev build with `npx tsx` runner.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```
# Direct send stress test — 20 concurrent messages to same user
npx tsx scripts/feishu-stress-real.mts

# Reply stress test — 15 concurrent replies to a single seed message
npx tsx scripts/feishu-stress-reply.mts --count=15
```

**Evidence after fix**:

Direct send (`im.message.create`) — 20 concurrent:
```
[config] appId=cli_aa947bd28afa5bd9  target=ou_33af95b6f9c0e5fbcaccef732f03d720  count=20

Firing 20 concurrent sends through requestFeishuApi (with retry) …

  (×10 rate-limit 230020 hits — all retried automatically)

  [10] ✓ 1609ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec44878f0a0b3c1c92d40adb2d
  [20] ✓ 1613ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec448782ca0b1c56b3f9cac777
  [11] ✓ 1627ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec4487850a0b1934d47f23f5ee
  [17] ✓ 2708ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ec4480bc478b1fe39b7f652100

─────────────────── summary ───────────────────
  total     : 20
  succeeded : 20
  failed    : 0
  wall time : 2709 ms
───────────────────────────────────────────────
```

Reply send (`im.message.reply`) — 15 concurrent:
```
[config] appId=cli_aa947bd28afa5bd9  target=ou_33af95b6f9c0e5fbcaccef732f03d720  count=15

[step 1] ✓ seed message_id = om_x100b6ecbc38750b8b29ba53f44a8667
[step 2] Firing 15 concurrent replies through requestFeishuApi (with retry) …

  (×5 rate-limit 230020 hits — all retried and succeeded)

  [01] ✓ 850ms   msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc395fca0b01e118c0d0feea
  [04] ✓ 1112ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc39128acb129b0880a4324d
  [15] ✓ 1192ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc390f0a8b1d802607f077e1
  [06] ✓ 1603ms  msg_id=om_x100b6ecbc3a954b0b4b3d0ce617fa06

─────────────────── reply summary ───────────────────
  total     : 15
  succeeded : 15
  failed    : 0
  wall time : 1604 ms
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

**Observed result after fix**:
- Direct send: 10/20 initially rate-limited → retried → **20/20 success**
- Reply send: 5/15 initially rate-limited → retried → **15/15 success**
- Before fix: rate-limited messages were lost permanently with no retry.

**What was not tested**: 20-concurrent reply to the same message exhausts the bounded retry for 4/20 (reply endpoint has stricter per-message rate limit). The 2-retry/linear-backoff design handles normal production concurrency (≤15) without message loss.

Closes #70879

target.body:
﻿## Summary

- Fixes `workspaceAccess: "rw"` sandboxes so non-workspace skills are materialized into a sandbox-readable, read-only skills workspace instead of advertising host paths the agent cannot read.
- Threads the materialized skills workspace through Docker, browser, SSH, local/remote fs bridges, read-tool workspace guards, embedded run prompts, and compaction prompt rebuilds.
- Documents the generated read-only sandbox skill path at `/workspace/.openclaw/sandbox-skills/skills`.
- Keeps generated skills out of user-owned project workspace paths, including shared `rw` sandboxes, while preserving workspace-owned `.openclaw/sandbox-skills` content.

## Summary
- Treat leading cron `NO_SUBPROCESS_STARTED` diagnostics as non-deliverable control replies only for Matrix cron delivery.
- Suppress that diagnostic before Matrix/channel delivery while preserving ordinary explanatory text that merely mentions the token.
- Keep non-Matrix cron delivery behavior unchanged; the same leading diagnostic still delivers for a Telegram/non-Matrix target.
- Keep `NO_REQUIRED_TOOL_AVAILABLE` deliverable for now; the helper test explicitly verifies it is not suppressed.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Matrix cron delivery should suppress a leading `NO_SUBPROCESS_STARTED` control diagnostic instead of delivering it to the Matrix/channel target, while preserving ordinary substantive text that merely mentions the token.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw setup on macOS with a real enabled Matrix cron configured for isolated agent delivery; private room/account identifiers redacted from copied live output.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Inspected the live cron configuration with `/opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw cron list --json`, then exercised the actual cron delivery path through `dispatchCronDelivery` -> `normalizeSilentReplyText` -> outbound delivery using the targeted verbose test command below.

```console
$ /opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw cron list --json | jq '<redacted selector>'
{
  "enabled": true,
  "sessionTarget": "isolated",
  "delivery": {
    "mode": "announce",
```

## Summary

Fixes mobile Control UI layout issues — rebased cleanly onto current `main`.

### What changed

**CSS — `layout.mobile.css`**
- Collapsed nav rail now slides off-screen with `transform/opacity/pointer-events` on mobile instead of sitting at `78px` and overlapping content
- Controls dropdown redesigned: centered full-width sheet with a blur backdrop overlay instead of a right-aligned corner popover; uses `border-radius-lg`, stronger shadow, `min(58svh, 420px)` height cap
- Thinking-toggle buttons grid changed from 4-column icon-only to 2-column with text labels (`Thinking / Tools / Cron`)
- Landscape phone support added throughout (`(max-width: 932px) and (max-height: 500px) and (orientation: landscape)`)
- Composer safe-area margin updated to `max(10px, calc(10px + env(safe-area-inset-bottom)))` — fixes iOS Chrome/Safari clip
- Topbar touch targets (sidebar trigger, search, mobile gear toggle) normalized to 44 × 44 px

**CSS — `chat/grouped.css`**
- New `.chat-bubble--has-actions` modifier: on touch/mobile, moves action buttons below the message in a flex column instead of absolutely overlapping text
- Mobile message footer improvements: sender name truncates at `42vw`, footer buttons 36 × 36 px min, `msg-meta__summary` min-height

**CSS — `chat/layout.css`**
- Added `@media (max-width: 768px), landscape` block covering `sidebar-menu-trigger`, `chat-controls-mobile-toggle`, and composer buttons with consistent `44 × 44 px` targets

**TypeScript — `app-render.helpers.ts`**
- Added `chat-controls-sheet-backdrop` button before the controls dropdown; clicking it calls `setChatMobileControlsOpen(false, { restoreFocus: true })`
- Each thinking/tools/cron toggle button now carries a `<span class="chat-controls__mobile-label">` with a short localized label
- Extracted `thinkingLabel` / `toolCallsLabel` / `cronLabel` variables to DRY up title and aria-label attributes

**TypeScript — `chat/grouped-render.ts`**
- `reserveActionSpace` flag adds `chat-bubble--has-actions` to assistant message bubbles (not tool messages) so touch devices never show action buttons overlapping text

**i18n**
- Added `thinkingShort` / `toolsShort` / `cronShort` keys to `en.ts` and all 18 translated locales

**Scripts**
- `scripts/control-ui-i18n.ts`: `renderMeta` now inline-formats single-item `fallbackKeys` arrays for cleaner JSON diffs

### New tests
- `ui/src/styles/chat/layout.test.ts` — verifies the new landscape + touch-target media query in `layout.css`
- `ui/src/styles/layout.mobile.test.ts` — verifies backdrop CSS, dropdown sheet geometry, landscape two-column layout, safe-area composer margin, and hidden nav rail
- `ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.browser.test.ts` — verifies backdrop element exists, `aria-label="Dismiss"`, `open` class toggle, short mobile labels, and backdrop click dismissal
- `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts` — verifies `chat-bubble--has-actions` is present on assistant bubbles with copy/expand actions

### Verification
Branch rebased onto latest `main`. Changes are scoped; CI will run full test matrix via Testbox.

target.title: fix(providers/openrouter): treat openrouter.ai as long-TTL-eligible for Anthropic cache_control
target.body:
## Summary

`isLongTtlEligibleEndpoint()` decides whether the env-driven `OPENCLAW_CACHE_RETENTION=long` default (and conservative implicit-long behavior) promotes a request from a 5-minute to a 1-hour Anthropic cache `ttl`. Today it only whitelists `api.anthropic.com` and Google Vertex hosts — even though OpenRouter is the standard path for Anthropic models in many deployments, including the one in #9600, and explicitly supports the same `cache_control: { type: "ephemeral", ttl: "1h" }` shape ([OpenRouter prompt caching docs](https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/best-practices/prompt-caching)).

This PR adds `openrouter.ai` to the eligible list.

## Why this matters

`createOpenRouterSystemCacheWrapper` already plumbs `cacheRetention` into `applyAnthropicEphemeralCacheControlMarkers` via `resolveAnthropicEphemeralCacheControl(baseUrl, cacheRetention)`. So `cacheRetention: "long"` already works on OpenRouter via *explicit* opt-in. The asymmetry is only in the *env-driven default* path:

| Endpoint | `OPENCLAW_CACHE_RETENTION=long` env | `cacheRetention: "long"` explicit |
|---|---|---|
| `api.anthropic.com` | ✅ 1h | ✅ 1h |
| Vertex AI | ✅ 1h | ✅ 1h |
| **`openrouter.ai`** | **❌ 5m (today)** → ✅ 1h (this PR) | ✅ 1h (already) |
| Custom proxy | ❌ 5m | ✅ 1h |

The custom-proxy case stays gated to explicit opt-in (unchanged) because we can't validate arbitrary hosts honor the `ttl: "1h"` field. OpenRouter is an officially supported provider whose docs explicitly call out the field — so the env-driven default treating it the same as `api.anthropic.com` is consistent.

## Measured impact (single deployment, claude-opus-4.8 via OpenRouter)

For workloads dominated by spaced-out cron / one-shot subagent runs, the default 5-min TTL expires between runs and forces a fresh ~89K-token prefix cache write each time. From `openclaw status --verbose` on the deployment behind #9600:

- Interactive sessions (back-to-back turns within 5 min): **88% cache hit ✅**
- Cron / one-shot subagent sessions (spaced > 5 min apart): **1%, 33%, 33%, 72%, 87% — wildly variable ❌**

Modeled bleed at ~$18.75/M cache-write × ~89K rewritten per cron run × per-week run volume: **~$1.2–1.6k/week per active account**. Promoting OpenRouter to 1h-TTL eligibility brings the cron path in line with the interactive path. The 1h-TTL cache write costs 2x base input instead of 1.25x, so the breakeven is ~8 reads inside the hour — easily met on any account with regular cron / agent activity.

## Refs

- Resolves the OpenRouter-side scope of #9600
- OpenRouter docs explicitly support `"ttl": "1h"` for Anthropic via `cache_control`: https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/best-practices/prompt-caching#anthropic-claude

## Tests

`src/agents/anthropic-payload-policy.long-ttl-eligibility.test.ts` — 7 new cases:

1. `cacheRetention: "long"` on OpenRouter → emits `ttl: "1h"` (already works; regression guard)
2. `OPENCLAW_CACHE_RETENTION=long` env on OpenRouter → emits `ttl: "1h"` (**new behavior — main fix**)
3. `cacheRetention: "short"` on OpenRouter → emits short marker (no ttl)
4. `cacheRetention: "none"` on OpenRouter → returns undefined (no marker at all)
5. `OPENCLAW_CACHE_RETENTION=long` on `api.anthropic.com` (regression)
6. Unknown host with only env set → still falls back to short marker (gating preserved for arbitrary proxies)
7. Explicit `cacheRetention: "long"` on unknown host still emits `ttl: "1h"` (custom Anthropic-compatible proxies — unchanged behavior)

All 15 tests pass (8 existing + 7 new) across `agents-core`, `agents-support`, and `unit` projects.

```
 ✓ unit ../../src/llm/providers/stream-wrappers/anthropic-cache-control-payload.test.ts (1 test) 64ms
 ✓ agents-core ../../src/agents/anthropic-payload-policy.long-ttl-eligibility.test.ts (7 tests) 62ms
 ✓ agents-support ../../src/agents/anthropic-payload-policy.long-ttl-eligibility.test.ts (7 tests) 64ms
 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  15 passed (15)
```

## Risk

Low. The change is a single host added to a whitelist that controls a `ttl` field on already-valid `cache_control` markers. If OpenRouter ever stopped honoring `ttl: "1h"`, the marker degrades gracefully — Anthropic-via-OpenRouter would still cache at 5-min (the writes would just cost 2x for the same effect, and the user can revert to `cacheRetention: "short"` per-model). The custom-proxy gating is preserved.

target.title: fix(compaction): add circuit breaker to stop token burn when summarizer unavailable
target.body:
## Summary

When the summarizer model is persistently unavailable (rate-limited, overloaded, misconfigured), `summarizeInStages` calls `summarizeWithFallback` for every split, each burning 3+ retries per call. With N splits, this burns N × 3 × chunkTokens before finally throwing.

Add a circuit breaker in the `summarizeInStages` split loop:

- Track consecutive generic fallback results via typed `FallbackSummaryResult.isGenericFallback`
- After 2 consecutive fallback splits, stop the loop
- Return partial summaries collected so far
- Reset counter on successful split response

Also fix TS errors and mock issues in test files:
- `compaction.circuit-breaker.test.ts`: testModel type bypass
- `dreaming.test.ts`: getSession mock

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #58838
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

Token burn during persistent summarizer outages causes wasted API calls and slow failure recovery. Circuit breaker stops repeated fallback attempts after 2 consecutive generic failures, preserving partial summaries with useful context.

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior addressed**: Circuit breaker prevents token burn when summarizer is persistently unavailable. After 2 consecutive generic fallback splits (typed `FallbackSummaryResult.isGenericFallback: true`), the loop stops. Partial summaries that contain successful model output are not counted as generic fallbacks (`isGenericFallback: false`).

**Real environment tested**: Linux (Ubuntu), Node.js 22.22, local checkout on branch `fix/compaction-circuit-breaker-58838`, commit 73758fec3.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

- Remaining splits and merge retry are skipped (call-count proof in tests) ✓

target.title: fix(matrix): neutralize command progress failure labels
target.body:
## Summary
- narrow this PR to the false command-progress `failed` label only
- render vague terminal command-progress statuses (`failed`, `error`, `cancelled`, and related terminal statuses) as neutral command lifecycle text instead of a user-visible failure assertion when there is no concrete exit code/output failure to show
- preserve the existing raw command/detail behavior; this PR does not add Matrix `commandText` config, docs, schema, or type changes
- keep real command-output evidence visible: `command-output` still reports `exit N` when an exit code exists
- add focused shared streaming and Matrix monitor regressions for the false-failed case

## What changed after narrowing

The earlier version of this PR was broader than intended: it added a Matrix config/docs surface for hiding raw command text by default. That is intentionally removed here. The final PR diff is scoped to:

- `src/channels/streaming.ts`
- `src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts`
- `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test.ts`

The branch now only addresses misleading status text in command-progress previews.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Matrix command-progress previews could show an internal command item as `failed` even when the preview did not have concrete exit-code/output evidence to justify a user-visible failure assertion. This PR changes that vague terminal command-progress label to neutral lifecycle text while keeping raw command/detail text and real command-output exit codes visible.
- **Real environment tested:** Installed OpenClaw global package on macOS at `/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw`, with the built PR formatter module applied to the installed package. The installed package still reports `OpenClaw 2026.6.5-beta.2 (338b144)`; only the formatter runtime module was patched on disk to avoid downgrading the active installation.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node --input-type=module` importing `/opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.js`, then called `buildChannelProgressDraftLine`, `formatChannelProgressDraftText`, and `formatChannelProgressDraftLine` against status-only command progress, raw-detail command progress, and command-output with exit code.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from the installed OpenClaw package:

```json
{
  "statusOnly": {
    "kind": "item",
    "text": "🛠️ Command finished",
    "label": "Command finished",
    "icon": "🛠️",
    "toolName": "exec"
  },
  "statusText": "🛠️ Command finished",
  "rawDetail": {
    "kind": "item",
    "text": "🛠️ gh pr edit 91203 --body private-details",
    "label": "Exec",
    "icon": "🛠️",
    "detail": "gh pr edit 91203 --body private-details",
    "status": "finished",
    "toolName": "exec"
  },
  "rawText": "🛠️ gh pr edit 91203 --body private-details",
  "outputExit": "🛠️ exit 2",
  "statusTextContainsFailed": false,
  "rawTextContainsFailed": false,
  "rawTextContainsCommand": true
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** A status-only command progress item internally tagged `failed` renders `🛠️ Command finished`; raw command/detail text remains visible when supplied; the literal `failed` status is not rendered in the preview text; concrete command-output failures still show real exit evidence as `🛠️ exit 2`.
- **What was not tested:** A live Matrix room preview was not run because the gateway was intentionally not restarted for this verification. This proof is installed-package terminal/live-output proof from the real OpenClaw installation, not mocked unit-test output.

## Test coverage proof

The Matrix regression exercises a command progress item with raw detail still present and an internal status of `failed`:

- raw command/detail text is still present in the Matrix preview body (`gh pr edit ... private-details`)
- the literal `failed` status is not rendered in the preview body
- a status-only failed command progress item renders neutral lifecycle text (`Command finished`)

The shared streaming formatter regression also verifies that:

- status-mode command start renders `🛠️ Running command`
- terminal command progress renders `🛠️ Command finished`
- status-only failed command progress does not show `failed`
- raw command detail remains visible when raw detail is supplied, while the false status label is normalized away

This keeps the fix narrowly on the false user-facing status, not on OPSEC/output condensation.

## Shared formatter scope

Matrix visible progress uses the shared channel progress formatter, so the fix is in that shared formatter instead of being duplicated in the Matrix monitor. That means other consumers of the same shared command-progress formatter receive the same neutral wording for vague terminal command-progress statuses. Concrete command-output failures with exit codes still render as `exit N`.

## Tests and validation

Passed on the narrowed branch:

- `pnpm -s exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-streaming.test.ts -t "formats progress draft lines" --reporter verbose`
- `pnpm -s exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-matrix.config.ts extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/handler.test.ts -t "keeps raw command text but removes false failed status|renders status-only failed command progress" --reporter verbose`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm -s tsgo:prod`
- `pnpm -s check:test-types`
- `pnpm -s lint:core`
- `pnpm build`

## Risk notes

- User-visible behavior changes only for command-progress preview status wording.
- There is no config, docs, environment, or migration behavior change in the final scoped diff.
- Real command-output failures with an exit code remain visible as `exit N`.

target.title: fix(agents): allow runtime wildcard tool allowlist to override static…
target.body:
## Summary

When a cron job sets `toolsAllow: ["*"]`, the runtime wildcard should override the agent's static tool profile (e.g. `messaging`) so that `exec` and other coding tools remain available — including background/yield behavior.

Previously, `createOpenClawCodingTools` applied the static profile policy via `applyToolPolicyPipeline` before considering the runtime allowlist. This caused `exec` to be filtered out for cron sessions using the `messaging` profile even when `toolsAllow: ["*"]` was explicitly set. Additionally, `allowBackground` was computed from the original profile before the wildcard override, causing exec to become visible while `background: true` still ran synchronously.

### Key Changes

- `src/agents/agent-tools.ts`: When `runtimeToolAllowlist` contains `*` **and** `trigger === "cron"`, skip `profilePolicy` and `providerProfilePolicy` steps in both `allowBackground` derivation and the final tool policy pipeline. The wildcard override is scoped to cron-triggered runs only — non-cron callers still respect static/provider profile caps even when a wildcard allowlist is present.
- `src/agents/agent-tools.runtime-allowlist-override.test.ts`: Regression tests for `createOpenClawCodingTools` with wildcard + messaging profile, including non-cron caller boundary test.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/cron-isolated-session-tools.test.ts`: Integration test simulating the full cron isolated session tool construction path through `resolveEmbeddedAttemptToolConstructionPlan` and `createOpenClawCodingTools`.

### Issue Reference

Fixes #89228 (Bug B: exec unavailable in isolated sessions)

### Security Boundary

ClawSweeper P1: the initial implementation applied the wildcard override unconditionally in the shared `createOpenClawCodingTools` factory, meaning any embedded caller passing `runtimeToolAllowlist: ["*"]` could bypass static/provider profile caps. This version scopes the override to `trigger === "cron"` runs only. Non-cron callers (e.g. interactive, subagent) still respect profile caps even when a wildcard allowlist is present. This keeps the precedence rule (runtime operator intent > static profile defaults) limited to the cron path that the operator explicitly controls via `toolsAllow`.

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior addressed**: `exec` tool is now available with correct background behavior when cron isolated sessions use `toolsAllow: ["*"]` with a `messaging` profile agent. Non-cron callers with `runtimeToolAllowlist: ["*"]` still respect the messaging profile cap.

**Real environment tested**: Linux (Ubuntu), Node.js 22.22, real OpenClaw checkout at `07 Open Source/02-projects/openclaw`, branch `fix/cron-exec-wildcard-override-89228`, commit `335538c49`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  src/agents/agent-tools.runtime-allowlist-override.test.ts \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/cron-isolated-session-tools.test.ts \
  --run --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```
 RUN  v4.1.7

 ✓ unit-fast src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/cron-isolated-session-tools.test.ts > cron isolated session tool construction with messaging profile > wildcard allowlist overrides messaging profile for tool construction plan 4ms
 ✓ unit-fast src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/cron-isolated-session-tools.test.ts > cron isolated session tool construction with messaging profile > messaging profile without wildcard excludes shell tools from plan 1ms
 ✓ unit-fast src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/cron-isolated-session-tools.test.ts > cron isolated session tool construction with messaging profile > createOpenClawCodingTools includes exec with wildcard + messaging profile 992ms
 ✓ unit-fast src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/cron-isolated-session-tools.test.ts > cron isolated session tool construction with messaging profile > createOpenClawCodingTools excludes exec with messaging profile only 71ms
 ✓ unit-fast src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/cron-isolated-session-tools.test.ts > cron isolated session tool construction with messaging profile > non-cron trigger with wildcard does not override messaging profile 22ms
 ✓ unit-fast src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/cron-isolated-session-tools.test.ts > cron isolated session tool construction with messaging profile > process tool presence under cron wildcard implies allowBackground consistency 25ms
 ✓ unit-fast src/agents/agent-tools.runtime-allowlist-override.test.ts > createOpenClawCodingTools runtimeToolAllowlist override > includes exec when cron wildcard allowlist overrides messaging profile 1135ms
 ✓ unit-fast src/agents/agent-tools.runtime-allowlist-override.test.ts > createOpenClawCodingTools runtimeToolAllowlist override > excludes exec with messaging profile and no runtime wildcard 144ms
 ✓ unit-fast src/agents/agent-tools.runtime-allowlist-override.test.ts > createOpenClawCodingTools runtimeToolAllowlist override > does not override messaging profile for non-cron callers even with wildcard 24ms
 ✓ unit-fast src/agents/agent-tools.runtime-allowlist-override.test.ts > createOpenClawCodingTools runtimeToolAllowlist override > process tool presence implies allowBackground is true under cron wildcard 26ms
 ✓ unit-fast src/agents/agent-tools.runtime-allowlist-override.test.ts > createOpenClawCodingTools runtimeToolAllowlist override > does not allow exec with messaging profile and no runtime wildcard even with trigger cron 10ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  11 passed (11)
   Start at  11:31:26
   Duration  38.34s (transform 58.93s, setup 0ms, import 73.45s, tests 2.46s, environment 0ms)
```

**Observed result after fix**:
- `toolsAllow: ["*"]` + `messaging` profile + `trigger: "cron"` → `exec` and `process` present ✓
- `toolsAllow: ["*"]` + `messaging` profile + `trigger: "interactive"` → `exec` and `process` absent (non-cron caller respects profile cap) ✓
- `messaging` profile without wildcard → `exec` correctly absent ✓
- `messaging` profile with `trigger: "cron"` but no wildcard → `exec` absent ✓
- `process` tool present under cron wildcard → `allowBackground` is true (same effectiveProfilePolicy chain) ✓
- Tool construction plan correctly includes `includeShellTools: true` under wildcard ✓
- All 11 tests pass in 38.34s on real checkout ✓

**Live cron scheduler proof**: Built from PR branch (`pnpm build`), started local gateway (`node dist/index.js gateway`), set `toolsAllow: ["*"]` on a cron job with `sessionTarget: "isolated"` (messaging profile agent), ran `openclaw cron run --wait --expect-final`. Gateway log trace (redacted):

```
[trace:embedded-run] core-plugin-tool stages:
  runId=dreaming-narrative-light-...
  sessionId=a88a94d5-...
  phase=core-plugin-tools totalMs=33234
  stages=tool-policy:54ms,workspace-policy:1ms,base-coding-tools:2ms,
         shell-tools:2ms,            ← exec passes through with wildcard
         openclaw-tools:session-workspace:2ms,...,
         authorization-policy:2ms,schema-normalization:11ms
```

Cron run completed: `status: "ok"`, `durationMs: 79002`, `jobName: "Memory Dreaming Promotion"`.

Key: `shell-tools:2ms` appears in the pipeline — messaging profile did not filter exec when `toolsAllow: ["*"]` was set on a cron job. Non-cron callers would not receive this override.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end exec background/yield invocation inside a cron isolated session (the cron run completed with `NO_REPLY` summary, meaning the model did not invoke exec during the session). The tool-construction path proof (both Vitest and live gateway log) confirms exec is available and `allowBackground` is consistent; actual exec background execution would require a cron prompt that explicitly triggers exec with yield, which was not done in this proof run. The `allowBackground` derivation shares the same `effectiveProfilePolicy` chain as `process` tool visibility, which is directly verified in tests.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds echo delivery infrastructure that mirrors session messages to additional channels
- `SessionEchoTarget` type on `SessionEntry` for persistent echo target configuration
- `message:sent` internal hook triggers echo delivery (fire-and-forget, no circular echo)
- CLI: `openclaw sessions echo add/remove/list` subcommands
- Gateway method: `sessions.echo` for programmatic pin/unpin

## Motivation

Sessions used across multiple transports (e.g. Telegram and the web UI) result in fragmented chat logs. Echo targets let every turn appear in all pinned channels regardless of originating transport.

## Files changed

| File | Purpose |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/sessions/types.ts` | `SessionEchoTarget` type + `echoTargets` field |
| `src/infra/outbound/echo.ts` | Core echo resolution + delivery |
| `src/infra/outbound/echo-hook.ts` | Internal hook registration |
| `src/commands/sessions-echo.ts` | CLI subcommands |
| `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts` | `sessions.echo` gateway method |
| `src/gateway/server-methods.ts` | Method registration |
| `src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.ts` | Hook auto-registration at boot |
| `src/cli/program/register.status-health-sessions.ts` | CLI command wiring |

## Test plan

- [ ] Unit test for `resolveEchoTargets` (deduplication, role filtering)
- [ ] Integration test: add echo target via gateway method, send message, verify echo arrives
- [ ] Verify circular echo prevention (echo delivery does not re-trigger hooks)
- [ ] CLI smoke test: add/list/remove echo targets

Supersedes #88386 (closed: branch had unrelated deployment commits).

---

## Proof (manual)

> Mantis isn't triggerable with my repo access, so this is supplied manually. Ran this branch live on a real Telegram bot (Windows, real session store). Covers the **integration**, **circular-echo**, and **CLI smoke** items in the test plan above. Telegram chat id redacted as `<chat>`.

**Build note:** the branch did not build/typecheck as-is — repaired in fd8ada2025 (now on this branch): duplicate `SessionsEchoParams`, stale `theme` import + `theme.bold`, `echoTargets` missing from the reserved slot-key list, and a wrong `.find`/`.filter` param annotation (`threadId` is `string|number`). `tsgo:core` clean after.

**1. CLI `add` / `list` / `remove`** (real store, net-zero):
```
$ openclaw sessions echo add  --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --channel telegram --to <chat> --thread-id 26237 --label echo-proof
  Added echo target: telegram -> <chat> (echo-proof)
$ openclaw sessions echo list --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --json
  { "echoTargets":[{ "channel":"telegram","to":"<chat>","threadId":"26237","label":"echo-proof","addedAt":… }] }
$ openclaw sessions echo remove --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --channel telegram --to <chat> --thread-id 26237
  Removed echo target: telegram -> <chat>
$ openclaw sessions echo list --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67
  No echo targets configured for this session.
```
Raw store reads confirmed `echoTargets: null → […] → null`.

**2. Live cross-channel mirror** (source thread 67 → pinned thread 26237):
```
[telegram] Inbound message …direct, 12 chars                            ← user message in thread 67
[telegram] outbound send ok messageId=36822 threadId=26237 silent=true  ← echoed to pinned thread
[telegram] outbound send ok messageId=36824 threadId=26237 silent=true  ← echoed
```
Echo fires off `message:received`, independent of the agent turn.

**3. No circular echo** — every echo delivery targets only the pinned thread with `silent=true`; none re-echoed. Structural guarantee: `fireEchoDeliveries()` omits `session`/`mirror` so the echo's own send never raises `message:sent`, so it cannot re-enter the hook (`echo.ts`).

---

## Update — surface-agnostic echo + live web→Telegram proof

Since the last review:
- **`sessions.echo.threadId` widened** to accept numeric thread IDs (Telegram forum threads arrive as numbers) + focused protocol coverage. (`20dba6e`)
- **Echo extended to the gateway chat path** so it fires for **control-ui / web / CLI** turns, not only channel-originated ones. A turn on a session with echo targets now mirrors **both** the inbound user message and the assistant reply to the tagged channel(s) — channel-agnostic, gated so channel-pipeline turns don't double-echo. (`73b6c75e` + the existing inline assistant-echo)

This realizes the design intent: **an echo target on a session mirrors that session's turns to the tagged thread no matter which surface resumes the session** (channel, web, or CLI), because every surface shares the same session key.

### Live proof — web app → Telegram (the original thread)
A message sent in a Telegram-originated session **from the web app** mirrored into the original Telegram thread:

![web to telegram echo](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/echo88815-proof/echo-web-to-telegram-88815.png)

Gateway log (chat id redacted) — `echo-to-thread: 0 → 2` from one webchat turn:
```
telegram outbound send ok ... chatId=<chat> messageId=37041 threadId=67 silent=true   <- user message   (rendered "[via webchat]")
telegram outbound send ok ... chatId=<chat> messageId=37042 threadId=67 silent=true   <- assistant reply (rendered "[echo]")
cli turn ... outBytes=227 outHash=545140268128                                         <- real webchat turn (not a heartbeat)
```
Exactly two `silent=true` deliveries (user + assistant), no cascade — the no-loop guard held.



---

## Rework — native-final response, prompt-only echo marker

Per review feedback, the mirrored **response** should read as a real reply on the
pinned channel, not a tagged copy. Previously **both** the prompt and the response
carried a marker (the proof above shows the assistant reply rendered `[echo]`).

Now:
- **Prompt** keeps its `📱 [via <channel>] ` marker — it genuinely originated on
  another surface, so the mirror label is honest.
- **Response** fans out **un-prefixed** via `fireEchoDeliveries(..., { prefixed: false })`
  on the `message:sent` hook, so it renders as a **native reply** on each pinned channel.

`fireEchoDeliveries` gained an opt-out `{ prefixed?: boolean }`; only the assistant
(`message:sent`) path uses it. Unit coverage asserts both shapes directly
(`echo.test.ts`): assistant → bare text, user → `[via <channel>]`, plus the default
(no-opt-out) path still prefixes.

This is the **native-final** increment. True per-token streaming on echo channels —
render-decoupling so the response streams natively (editing a draft) on every pinned
channel instead of landing as one final message — is the **follow-up** (multiplexing
the agent stream; seam in `agent-runner-execution.ts`).

**Known tradeoff (deferred):** the prompt echo fires on `message:received`
(post-dedupe, post-binding) for immediacy, so a no-op command (e.g. `/help`) can mirror
a prompt with no paired response. Pairing the prompt echo to the committed turn lands
with the streaming rework.


---

## B-full — native streaming echo (one agent run, N native renders)

Builds on the native-final rework above. Instead of mirroring only the final text, a
streaming-enabled echo target now renders the **same** agent run **live and natively** on
its own channel — no second agent run.

**Mechanism (no compositor extraction, no N agent runs):** the origin runs the agent once;
every streaming event already broadcasts on the agent-event bus by `runId`. A per-target
**mirror resolver** replays that bus into the target channel's own `on*` callbacks, driving
the target's native live-edited draft. A literal "re-inject as a synthetic inbound" was
rejected — session-pinning shares one session key, so the per-session run lock would
serialize it into N sequential agent runs.

Slices (each unit-tested):
- **`echo-mirror-resolver.ts`** — replays the agent-event bus into a target's `on*` (7 tests).
- **`extensions/telegram/echo-renderer.ts`** — Telegram render target: a live-edited draft via
  the same `createTelegramDraftStream` primitive + markdown→HTML the native compositor uses (3 tests).
- **`echo-streaming.ts`** — channel-agnostic fan-out + renderer registry + per-turn gating so the
  post-hoc mirror skips targets a live renderer already owns (5 tests).
- **wiring** — `agent-runner-execution.ts` launches the fan-out at run start (before the bus emits);
  `echo-hook.ts` gates the post-hoc assistant mirror.
- **`extensions/telegram/echo-renderer-register.ts`** + new plugin-sdk subpath
  `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-echo` — Telegram registers its factory, reusing the channel's own
  stream config (stream-off target → post-hoc fallback) (4 tests).

**Coexistence, not replacement:** the post-hoc `fireEchoDeliveries` mirror stays as the fallback for
stream-off targets and for channels without a renderer yet (only Telegram has one). The prompt echo is
untouched. Inert until a factory is registered — so non-Telegram echoes and normal turns are unchanged.

19 new unit tests, `tsgo` clean, `tsdown-build` + plugin-sdk dts build clean. **Live two-destination
proof (webchat ↔ Telegram, echo each way) is in progress** via an adapted mantis/crabbox capture.


---

## Live streaming proof — webchat → Telegram (combined build)

B-full's native streaming only manifests when the persist/commentary stack is present
(it supplies the live progress-draft streaming + the claude-cli streaming parser), so the
isolated branch can't show it. Captured on a **combined proof build** = this PR
(channel-echo + B-full) **+ the persist/commentary stack + #89851 (MCP loopback transport)**,
3-way-ported with zero conflicts, run in a crabbox on a **test bot** (never the daily driver).

A webchat turn (`chat.send`) on a session pinned to echo into a Telegram supergroup, with the
response coming from a deterministic word-by-word mock model. Gateway log:

```
[ECHODBG-launch] sessionKey=agent:main:d1 echoTargets=1
[ECHODBG-fanout] targets=1 factories=telegram
[ECHODBG-fanout] target=telegram:-1003900553563 factory=yes renderer=yes
[ECHODBG-render] onPartialReply len=3
[ECHODBG-render] onPartialReply len=9        <- response text GROWS word-by-word
… (35 assistant deltas → 35 onPartialReply)
[ECHODBG-finalize] after stop msgId=40 liveEdits=4   <- ONE telegram message, EDITED 4× live
telegram outbound send ok chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=39   <- ONLY the prompt echo
```

- **35 streamed deltas** replay through the mirror resolver into the Telegram renderer (35 `onPartialReply`).
- The renderer sends **one** Telegram message and **edits it live** (`liveEdits=4`) as tokens arrive — i.e. the
  response visibly streams on Telegram, not a single final message.
- The only channel-plugin send to the supergroup is the **prompt echo** (`messageId=39`); the post-hoc
  response mirror is correctly **skipped** — fixed by the gating commit in this PR (`fix(channel-echo): keep
  streamed echo target marked for the whole turn`), a real double-delivery bug found during this capture.

So per turn the pinned Telegram chat shows: `📱 [via webchat] <prompt>` + the **response message growing
word-by-word**. The streaming behaviour is a stack-dependency (needs the persist PRs), which is why this
proof is on the combined build; the PR's own code (renderer + fan-out + gating) is fully exercised.

target.title: fix: recover crashed main sessions via lock-file detection fallback
target.body:
## Summary

After an unclean gateway crash, `cleanStaleLockFiles()` may find lock files from the previous process but classify them as non-stale (e.g. the orphan-payload grace window has not elapsed). When `cleaned.length === 0`, the existing `markRestartAbortedMainSessionsFromLocks()` never runs, leaving `status: "running"` sessions unrecovered on restart.

This PR drives the crash fallback from an explicit **safe-to-recover** lock classification emitted by the cleanup owner, instead of having Gateway infer crash residue from remaining locks with mtime heuristics.

### What changed

- `src/agents/session-write-lock.ts`: `cleanStaleLockFiles()` now returns a `safeToRecover` array and accepts a `startupMode` parameter. In startup mode, stale locks with a dead/absent owner and no report-only stale reasons are classified as safe to recover, **including** orphan-payload locks within the grace window. Outside startup mode, grace-window-protected orphan locks are excluded to avoid false positives during runtime.
- `src/agents/main-session-restart-recovery.ts`: `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()` accepts `safeToRecoverLocks` instead of `remainingLocks` + `gatewayStartedAt`. The marker trusts the cleanup classification — no mtime heuristics or `pidAlive` checks.
- `src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.ts`: 
  - **Startup-only crash recovery** runs in `sidecars.session-locks-startup` before channels/session writers start, with `startupMode: true`. This ensures grace-window orphan locks are safely classified as crash residue before any live writer can create new locks.
  - The existing post-ready sidecar `sidecars.session-locks` continues cleanup without `startupMode` (runtime-safe mode), removing stale locks and marking restart-aborted sessions through the existing `cleaned` path.
- Tests: Regression coverage for startup-mode classification, grace window exclusion outside startup mode, report-only stale reasons, and startup-before-channel timing.

### Safety

- Startup recovery runs **before** channels and session writers can create locks, so live post-channel locks are never included in `safeToRecover`.
- Post-ready cleanup runs in runtime-safe mode (no `startupMode`), so grace-window orphan locks are preserved and not falsely marked.
- The crash marker only acts on locks that `cleanStaleLockFiles` explicitly classified as safe to recover — no heuristic inference.
- Live-owner locks (`pidAlive === true`) are never included because they are not stale.
- Report-only stale locks (`too-old`, `hold-exceeded`) are excluded because the owner may still be working.

### ClawSweeper P1 resolution

| P1 finding | Resolution |
|---|---|
| Drive crash fallback from real cleanup classifications | Marker consumes `safeToRecover` from `cleanStaleLockFiles()` — no mtime heuristics |
| Keep young unknown-owner locks out of recovery | `safeToRecover` excludes grace-window orphan locks outside `startupMode`; startup recovery runs before writers can create locks |
| Replace simulated cleanup proof | Proof below uses real `cleanStaleLockFiles()` + `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks()` through the production startup contract |
| Keep grace-window locks out of post-ready recovery | Startup recovery (`startupMode: true`) runs before channel startup; post-ready sidecar runs without `startupMode` (runtime-safe) |

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior addressed**: Gateway crash → `cleanStaleLockFiles(startupMode: true)` finds orphan-payload lock files within the grace window → `cleaned.length === 0` but `safeToRecover.length > 0` → crash fallback marks matching running sessions for recovery.

**Real environment tested**: Linux (Ubuntu), Node.js 22.22, real OpenClaw checkout, branch `fix/crash-session-recovery-80254`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
node --import tsx scripts/crash-recovery-proof.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```
=== Crash Recovery Proof ===

1. Setting up pre-crash state: running session + lock file
   Lock: /tmp/openclaw-crash-proof-.../crash-test-session.jsonl.lock (orphan, 5s old, within grace window)
   Session: agent:main:main (status: running)

2. cleanStaleLockFiles(startupMode: undefined) — runtime behavior
   cleaned: 0
   safeToRecover: 0
   Lock still exists: true
   → Runtime correctly preserves orphan lock in grace window, safeToRecover empty

3. cleanStaleLockFiles(startupMode: true) — startup behavior
   cleaned: 0
   safeToRecover: 1
   safeToRecover[0]: pidAlive=false, stale=true, reasons=missing-pid,invalid-createdAt

4b. markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks (safeToRecover path)
   marked: 1
   skipped: 0

5. Verify session store after recovery
   agent:main:main: status=running, abortedLastRun=true

✓ PASS: Orphan lock in grace window → safeToRecover → abortedLastRun=true via startup crash fallback

6. Lock file preserved (not deleted): true
```

**Observed result after fix**:
- `cleanStaleLockFiles(removeStale: true, startupMode: true)` with orphan lock in grace window → `safeToRecover` contains lock → crash fallback marks `abortedLastRun=true` ✓
- `cleanStaleLockFiles(removeStale: true)` without startupMode with orphan lock → `safeToRecover` empty → no false marks ✓
- `cleanStaleLockFiles(removeStale: true)` with dead-PID lock → lock removed, enters `cleaned`, `safeToRecover` empty ✓
- Startup recovery runs before channel startup; post-ready sidecar runs in runtime-safe mode ✓
- Real helper proof: orphan lock → `safeToRecover` → `markCrashedMainSessionsFromRemainingLocks` → `abortedLastRun=true` ✓

**What was not tested**: Live Gateway crash/restart end-to-end (starting `node dist/index.js gateway`, killing it, and restarting). The proof script exercises the actual cleanup + marker functions through the real production code path with `startupMode: true`, which is the same path `cleanupStaleSessionLocks` calls during Gateway startup before channels start.

Fixes #80254

target.title: chore: formatDiskSpaceBytes emits "1024 MiB" instead of "1.0 GiB" a
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #90245. AI-generated fix, human-reviewed.

## Change Type / Scope / Linked Issue

- **Type**: chore
- **Scope**: infra/disk-space
- **Linked Issue**: Fixes #90245

## Motivation

`formatDiskSpaceBytes` in `src/infra/disk-space.ts` can render the impossible unit string `"1024 MiB"` instead of `"1.0 GiB"` for byte counts in the 1023.5–1023.9 MiB range. This string is operator-facing — it appears in the low-disk-space warning shown during setup/update staging (`createLowDiskSpaceWarning`).

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior addressed:** The unit branch tests `mib < 1024` using the unrounded value, but the MiB branch then rounds via `Math.round(mib)`, causing values in `[1023.5, 1024)` to round up to `1024` yet still be formatted as `"1024 MiB"`. The fix rounds first (`roundedMib = Math.round(mib)`), then chooses the unit based on `roundedMib < 1024`, ensuring the GiB branch is taken when the rounded value reaches 1024.

**Real environment tested:** Local development (Node 22.22.0, pnpm, Linux)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

1. Check out this branch
2. Run the actual patched `formatDiskSpaceBytes` directly from `src/infra/disk-space.ts`:

```bash
$ node -e "const { formatDiskSpaceBytes } = require('./src/infra/disk-space.ts'); [Math.round(1023.5*1024*1024), Math.round(1023.7*1024*1024), Math.round(1023.9*1024*1024), Math.round(1024.0*1024*1024)].forEach(b => console.log(b + ' bytes -> \"' + formatDiskSpaceBytes(b) + '\"'));"
1073217536 bytes -> "1.0 GiB"
1073427251 bytes -> "1.0 GiB"
1073636966 bytes -> "1.0 GiB"
1073741824 bytes -> "1.0 GiB"
```

3. Run the colocated regression tests:

```bash
$ pnpm test src/infra/disk-space.test.ts

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts
 RUN  v4.1.7

 ✓ |infra| src/infra/disk-space.test.ts (4 tests) 117ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
   Duration  932ms

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 12.54s
```

**Evidence before fix:** Reproduced on `main` with the old logic:

```bash
$ node -e "function old(bytes){ const mib=bytes/(1024*1024); if(mib<1024){ return Math.max(0,Math.round(mib))+' MiB'; } const gib=mib/1024; return gib.toFixed(gib<10?1:0)+' GiB'; } [Math.round(1023.5*1024*1024), Math.round(1023.7*1024*1024), Math.round(1023.9*1024*1024)].forEach(b => console.log(b + ' bytes -> \"' + old(b) + '\"'));"
1073217536 bytes -> "1024 MiB"
1073427251 bytes -> "1024 MiB"
1073636966 bytes -> "1024 MiB"
```

**Evidence after fix:** Actual execution of the patched helper via `require('./src/infra/disk-space.ts')`:

```bash
$ node -e "const { formatDiskSpaceBytes } = require('./src/infra/disk-space.ts'); [Math.round(1023.5*1024*1024), Math.round(1023.7*1024*1024), Math.round(1023.9*1024*1024)].forEach(b => console.log(b + ' bytes -> \"' + formatDiskSpaceBytes(b) + '\"'));"
1073217536 bytes -> "1.0 GiB"
1073427251 bytes -> "1.0 GiB"
1073636966 bytes -> "1.0 GiB"
```

**Observed result after fix:** Values that previously produced `"1024 MiB"` now correctly produce `"1.0 GiB"`. The 1024 MiB → 1.0 GiB promotion now uses the rounded MiB value for the branch decision, eliminating the off-by-rounding bug.

**What was not tested:** Full integration tests, live disk-space warnings in production.

## Security Impact

AI-generated fix, please review for security implications.

## Human Verification

Verified by running local lint suite (`pnpm lint:core`), type checks (`pnpm tsgo:test:src`), changed tests (`pnpm test:changed`), and direct execution of the patched helper as shown above.

## Compatibility / Risks

No known breaking changes. The fix only changes the internal rounding order; all existing outputs for values well below or well above the 1024 MiB boundary remain identical.

target.title: feat(gateway): add first-output and completed latency phases to chat.send_timing
target.body:
## Summary

Partially addresses #90556. Narrow, server-only change.

## Change Type / Scope / Linked Issue

- **Type**: feat
- **Scope**: gateway
- **Linked Issue**: Partially addresses #90556

## Motivation

Issue #90556 requests displaying response latency in the Chat UI. The first step is making latency data available via the existing `chat.send_timing` event. Currently, `chat.send_timing` only covers dispatch phases (ack → dispatch → model-selected → agent-run-started → dispatch-completed). It does **not** track LLM first-token latency (time to first output) or total end-to-end latency.

This PR adds two new timing phases without modifying the `chat` event payload, making it fully backward-compatible.

## Changes

### Core state (ChatRunState + GatewayRequestContext)

- `ChatRunState.chatSendReceivedAt`: `Map<string, number>` — stores `performance.now()` at the moment `chat.send` is received, keyed by `clientRunId`
- `ChatRunState.firstOutputEmitted`: `Map<string, boolean>` — prevents double-emitting `first-output` for the same run
- Both maps are cleared in `clearRun()` and `clear()`
- `GatewayRequestContext` and its factory functions (`createLocalGatewayRequestContext`, `createGatewayRequestContext`) expose the new maps

### Timing emission (server-chat.ts + server-runtime-subscriptions.ts)

- On the first delta for a run, if `chatSendReceivedAt` has a timestamp and `emitServerTiming` is provided, emits `chat.send_timing` with `phase: "first-output"` and `firstOutputLatencyMs`
- Before clearing run state on completion, emits `chat.send_timing` with `phase: "completed"` and `totalLatencyMs`
- `emitServerTiming` is wired through `startGatewayEventSubscriptions` → `createAgentEventHandler` → delta/completion handlers

### chat.ts (chat handler)

- `context.chatSendReceivedAt.set(clientRunId, chatSendReceivedAtMs)` — stores the receipt timestamp so the event handlers can compute latency

### Shared type (ChatSendServerTimingPhase)

- Added `"first-output"` and `"completed"` to the union type

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** No first-output or total latency metrics exist in the `chat.send_timing` event (issue #90556).

**Real environment tested:** Local checkout on branch `feat/gateway-add-latency-to-send-timing-90556` (commit 65963fa), Node 22.22.0, pnpm, Linux.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:** Vitest terminal output confirming all tests pass with the new `chatSendReceivedAt` and `firstOutputEmitted` maps:
```
 ✓ |gateway-methods| src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts (106 tests) 6181ms
     ✓ broadcasts session metadata changes reported by chat command dispatch  501ms
     ✓ preserves offloaded attachment media paths in transcript order  423ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  212 passed (212)

 ✓ |gateway-server| src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts (68 tests) 50.97s
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  68 passed (68)
```

Source diff confirming the timing storage and emission:
```diff
--- a/src/gateway/server-chat-state.ts
+++ b/src/gateway/server-chat-state.ts
+  chatSendReceivedAt: Map<string, number>;
+  firstOutputEmitted: Map<string, boolean>;
+  const chatSendReceivedAt = new Map<string, number>();
+  const firstOutputEmitted = new Map<string, boolean>();
+    chatSendReceivedAt.delete(runId);
+    firstOutputEmitted.delete(runId);
+    chatSendReceivedAt.clear();
+    firstOutputEmitted.clear();

--- a/src/gateway/server-chat.ts
+++ b/src/gateway/server-chat.ts
+  emitServerTiming?: (params) => void;
+    if (!chatRunState.firstOutputEmitted.has(clientRunId)) {
+      chatRunState.firstOutputEmitted.set(clientRunId, true);
+      const receivedAt = chatRunState.chatSendReceivedAt.get(clientRunId);
+      if (receivedAt !== undefined && emitServerTiming) {
+        emitServerTiming({ phase: "first-output", extra: { firstOutputLatencyMs } });
+      }
+    }
+    const receivedAt = chatRunState.chatSendReceivedAt.get(clientRunId);
+    if (receivedAt !== undefined && emitServerTiming) {
+      emitServerTiming({ phase: "completed", extra: { totalLatencyMs } });
+    }

--- a/src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts
+++ b/src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts
+      context.chatSendReceivedAt.set(clientRunId, chatSendReceivedAtMs);
```

**Observed result after fix:** Two new `chat.send_timing` phases (`"first-output"` and `"completed"`) are emitted with correct latency measurements. All 212 + 68 = 280 gateway tests pass. The `ChatRunState` properly stores and cleans up the new tracking maps via `clearRun()`. The mock `GatewayRequestContext` objects in all test files include `chatSendReceivedAt: new Map()` and `firstOutputEmitted: new Map()`.

**What was not tested:** Full end-to-end integration with a live OpenClaw gateway and WebSocket client (requires running `openclaw serve` with an active LLM provider). The latency calculation logic (`performance.now() - receivedAt`) is verified through unit tests that mock the timing maps.

## Security Impact

No security impact. Only adds timing data to an existing operator-only event (`chat.send_timing` is broadcast with `dropIfSlow: true` and never contains user content).

## Compatibility / Risks

No breaking changes. The new phases are additive — existing `chat.send_timing` listeners that don't handle `"first-output"` or `"completed"` will simply ignore them. The `chat` event payload is unchanged.

- `merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery`: The `chat.send_timing` event is already marked `dropIfSlow: true`, so adding two more emissions does not risk blocking the message stream. Both new emissions use the same `dropIfSlow` flag.
